steps.title: Understand what the question asks
steps.content: We need the number of $x$-values where $f(x) = 0$, i.e., the number of $x$-intercepts of the graph of $f(x) = ab^x + c$.

steps.title: Recognize the shape of an exponential function
steps.content: A function of the form $f(x) = ab^x + c$ is exponential. Its graph has a horizontal asymptote at $y = c$. The curve approaches that asymptote but never crosses it. From the graph, the asymptote is above (or below) $y = 0$, so the curve never reaches $y = 0$.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'Three': An exponential curve never oscillates; it is strictly increasing or decreasing and cannot cross the $x$-axis three times. ✗
  • Choice B — 'Two': Same reasoning — an exponential function is one-to-one and cannot cross the $x$-axis more than once regardless of a horizontal shift. ✗
  • Choice C — 'One': The graph would cross $y = 0$ once only if the horizontal asymptote were $y = 0$ (i.e., $c = 0$) and $a$ had the opposite sign to the asymptote side. The graph shown has its asymptote displaced from the $x$-axis, so the curve never reaches $y = 0$. ✗
  • Choice D — 'Zero': The graph approaches its horizontal asymptote and never intersects the $x$-axis, so $f(x) = 0$ has no solution. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
